source.title: A new view of pain as a homeostatic emotion.

confidence_rationale: Craig's 2003 paper is the clearest published statement that pain (especially C-fiber mediated slow pain) functions as a homeostatic emotion: a motivational signal reflecting deviation from physiological homeostasis, analogous to hunger, thirst, and temperature. This directly supports the z_harm_a component of SD-011 as an accumulated homeostatic deviation signal (C-fiber / paleospinothalamic analog). Craig specifies that lamina I neurons receiving predominantly C-fiber input drive a pathway through VMpo thalamus to posterior insula that generates the homeostatic feeling, and then to ACC that generates the behavioral motivation. The model explicitly dissociates this from the sensory-discriminative pathway through VPL to S1/S2. Confidence is 0.86 rather than higher because: (a) this is a framework review not a new experiment; (b) the mapping between Craig's homeostatic allostasis framing and the specific claim that z_harm_a 'accumulates homeostatic deviation' is inferential rather than experimentally demonstrated.
failure_signatures: Craig's model treats pain as generated by a specific labeled-line system (lamina I -> VMpo -> insula -> ACC), not by distributed C-fiber activity whose integration over time accumulates harm. If the accumulation property is not a feature of the pathway architecture but is instead a secondary consequence of peripheral sensitization, the z_harm_a characterization as a running integral would be architecturally misleading.

The homeostatic emotion framework explicitly groups pain with temperature, itch, hunger, and thirst as a family of interoceptive emotions. This suggests z_harm_a and the homeostatic/allostatic drive signals in REE (SD-012) may be instantiating the same underlying mechanism from different angles -- potentially a scope conflict rather than independent support.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Pain is a homeostatic emotion: a specific sensation-plus-motivation generated by lamina I neurons (predominantly C-fiber input) whose axons ascend in the lateral spinothalamic tract to VMpo thalamus and project to posterior insula (generating the feeling of the body's physiological state) and to ACC (generating the affective-motivational behavioral drive). This pathway signals deviation from physiological homeostasis -- damage, inflammation, sustained noxious input -- and motivates corrective behavior, analogous to the way hunger signals metabolic deficit and motivates feeding. The pathway is anatomically and computationally distinct from the neospinothalamic pathway (A-delta, VPL, S1/S2) that encodes stimulus location and intensity.
mapping.ree_translation: Craig's homeostatic emotion framework provides the biological grounding for the z_harm_a component of SD-011. The C-fiber / lamina I / VMpo / insula / ACC pathway is the biological substrate of z_harm_a: it carries accumulated homeostatic deviation (damage, sustained noxious input, tissue state) as a motivational signal, not a sensory-discriminative one. Crucially, this pathway does NOT forward-predict -- it reflects the current state of the body, updated slowly by C-fiber traffic that integrates over time. This contrasts with the A-delta pathway (z_harm_s in SD-011), which is fast, localizable, and more suitable for forward modeling by E2_harm_s (ARC-033). Craig's model thus simultaneously supports the z_harm_s / z_harm_a stream separation AND the differential predictability claim that motivates ARC-033: z_harm_a is a homeostatic state signal that cannot be forward-modeled on short timescales in the same way z_harm_s can.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Craig's homeostatic emotion model groups pain under the same architectural umbrella as temperature, itch, hunger, and thirst -- all are C-fiber-mediated lamina I emotions. This creates a potential scope collision with SD-012 (homeostatic drive) in the REE architecture: if z_harm_a and the homeostatic drive signal are both expressions of the lamina I C-fiber pathway, they may be the same signal rather than distinct streams. REE needs to specify whether z_harm_a (harm-specific deviation) and the SD-012 drive_level (metabolic/homeostatic deviation) are computed in the same substrate or separate ones. Craig's framework would suggest a unified homeostatic substrate, which challenges the current REE architectural assumption that they are separable latents.
mapping.source_context: Theoretical review with anatomical and imaging evidence synthesis. Builds on Craig's 2002 Nature Reviews Neuroscience interoception paper (PMID 12154366). Published in Trends in Neurosciences. Highly cited (>1000 citations).

confidence_components.notes: High source quality (Trends Neurosci, highly cited, same author as the canonical interoception framework). Mapping fidelity is good -- the homeostatic emotion framing maps directly onto z_harm_a's design intention. Transfer risk is low-moderate: Craig's framework is about somatic nociception and body-state monitoring, not about harm-in-world as an ethical signal, which is what REE models. The functional logic transfers; the specific biology does not.
